An honest comparison of the best local coding AI models to help you choose the right one for your workflow in 2026.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Generated by ChatGPT A question keeps showing up in developer forums and group chats lately: do you still need the cloud for coding, or can a local AI model actually do the job now?
A year or two ago, the honest answer was “not really, not for anything serious.” That’s changed. Local coding models have closed a lot of the gap with cloud tools, and for certain workflows, they’ve become genuinely competitive rather than a compromise.
This isn’t a promotional roundup. It’s a practical comparison of seven local coding models worth knowing about right now, what each one is actually good at, what hardware they need, and where the cloud still wins.
What Is a Local LLM, and Why Bother?
A local LLM is a language model you download and run entirely on your own machine, instead of sending your code to a company’s servers through an API. Tools like Ollama and LM Studio make this fairly painless — you pull a model, and it runs behind a local server your editor can talk to.
The appeal is straightforward. Your code never leaves your machine, which matters a lot for proprietary codebases, regulated industries, or anyone who’s simply not comfortable pasting production code into a cloud chat window. There’s also no per-token bill — once you own the hardware, inference is essentially free, which adds up fast during heavy prototyping or automated code review.
📊 Cloud AI vs Local AI

The Honest Limitations
Local models still generally trail the very best cloud models — like Claude or GPT-class systems — on the hardest, most open-ended reasoning tasks. The best local models score respectably on benchmarks like SWE-bench Verified, but the top cloud models still tend to sit several points ahead on the most demanding problems.
Hardware is the other real constraint. A model that performs beautifully on a workstation with a 24GB GPU may be unusable on a laptop with 8GB of memory. Picking the right model means being honest about what your actual machine can run, not just what scores well on a leaderboard.
One thing that surprises many beginners is that most people don’t run the original model.
Instead, they use quantized versions like Q4 or Q5. Quantization reduces memory usage while preserving most of the model’s coding ability.
That’s why a model that originally needs around 40 GB of memory can often run comfortably on a 24 GB GPU.
How These Models Were Compared
Each model below is evaluated on coding quality, reasoning ability, debugging behavior, refactoring support, context length, generation speed, hardware requirements, and how easy it is to actually get running. Where benchmark numbers are self-reported by a model’s creator rather than confirmed by an independent leaderboard, that’s noted — some of the newest releases are still being independently verified.
1. Qwen3-Coder-Next (Alibaba)
Overview: A Mixture-of-Experts model that activates only a portion of its parameters per generation, which is what lets a model with over 100GB of total weights still run on a single 24GB GPU. <cite index=”14–1">It scores 58.7% on SWE-bench Verified with a 256K context window.</cite>
Strengths: Strong all-around agentic coding performance and a genuinely long context window that stays useful rather than degrading at the edges.
Weaknesses: <cite index=”14–1">It’s a heavier model, running around 18 to 22 tokens per second on a 4090</cite> — noticeably slower than lighter models on this list.
Recommended hardware: A single 24GB GPU, such as an RTX 4090.
Who should use it: Developers who want one solid default model for daily agentic coding work and have the GPU to support it.
2. GLM-4.7 Thinking (Zhipu AI)
Overview: An open-weight model that has consistently ranked near the top of independent coding leaderboards. <cite index=”21–1">On tracked benchmarks it posts an Artificial Analysis Intelligence Index of 51.1, a SWE-Bench Pro score of 62.1, and a LiveBench agentic score that beats every proprietary model tracked in that comparison.</cite>
Strengths: Excellent open-weight benchmark performance, particularly on agentic, multi-step tasks.
Weaknesses: Strong IDE tooling integration and English-language documentation still lag behind Western model ecosystems in places.
Recommended hardware: Higher-end setups; check current quantized releases for your specific GPU tier before committing.
Who should use it: Developers chasing the strongest open-weight benchmark numbers available and willing to work with a less mature Western tooling ecosystem.
3. DeepSeek-Coder V3 (Distilled)
Overview: A 16B dense model trained by distilling reasoning behavior from the larger DeepSeek V3.2 into a much smaller student model. <cite index=”14–1">It scores 40.5% on SWE-bench Verified while running on just 12GB of VRAM</cite> — the best quality-per-gigabyte on this list.
Strengths: Runs comfortably on modest consumer GPUs, like a 4070 Ti or a 12GB laptop GPU, while still retaining useful reasoning behavior from its larger parent model.
Weaknesses: Its ceiling on genuinely hard problems is lower than the flagship-sized models above it.
Recommended hardware: 12GB VRAM or more.
Who should use it: Developers on a single mid-range GPU who want the best coding performance that hardware tier can realistically support.
4. Gemma 4 31B IT QAT (Google)
Overview: A multimodal coding model, meaning it can work with screenshots of error messages, UI layouts, or architecture diagrams alongside plain code.
Strengths: Strong coding results paired with genuinely useful multimodal support — a real advantage when debugging visual issues or discussing a diagram instead of typing everything out.
Weaknesses: As a broader multimodal model, it isn’t quite as narrowly optimized for pure code generation as dedicated coding-only models.
Recommended hardware: A single high-VRAM consumer GPU; check current quantized builds for exact requirements.
Who should use it: Web and frontend developers who regularly debug from screenshots, or anyone who wants one model that handles both code and visual context.
5. Codestral 25.12 (Mistral)
Overview: A model built specifically around fast inline code completion, with native fill-in-the-middle support designed for real-time autocomplete rather than long conversational answers.
Strengths: <cite index=”14–1">Runs comfortably on 16GB VRAM</cite>, making it one of the more accessible models here, and it’s tuned specifically for the kind of instant suggestions you’d expect from GitHub Copilot.
Weaknesses: It’s built for completion, not deep multi-step reasoning — pair it with a stronger agentic model for harder debugging or architecture questions.
Recommended hardware: 16GB VRAM.
Who should use it: Developers who mainly want fast, low-latency autocomplete inside their editor rather than a conversational coding assistant.
6. Llama 4 Scout (Meta)
Overview: An older but still capable Mixture-of-Experts model, notable mainly for its context window. <cite index=”14–1">It supports a 10 million token context window, by far the largest in the open-weight space, and is also multimodal, able to read screenshots of error messages or architecture diagrams alongside code.</cite>
Strengths: Genuinely able to hold an entire mid-sized codebase in a single prompt, which is unmatched by any other model on this list.
Weaknesses: <cite index=”14–1">It scores 47.3% on SWE-bench Verified, putting it behind the dedicated coding models on raw patch quality.</cite>
Recommended hardware: Heavier hardware requirements given its size; a high-VRAM GPU or Apple Silicon with substantial unified memory.
Who should use it: Developers working on large, unfamiliar codebases who need to reason across many files simultaneously, more than they need top-tier patch accuracy.
7. Devstral Small 2 (Mistral)
Overview: A 24B dense model purpose-built for agentic coding — reading multi-file codebases, writing patches, calling tools, and self-correcting from failed tests. <cite index=”23–1">It’s fine-tuned specifically on agentic coding workflows: reading multi-file codebases, writing patches, running tool calls, and self-correcting from test failures.</cite>
Strengths: <cite index=”28–1">Requires approximately 18.9GB of VRAM with Q4_K_M quantization</cite>, putting real agentic coding ability within reach of a single high-end consumer GPU. <cite index=”27–1">It carries an Apache 2.0 license, allowing commercial use, modification, and redistribution without significant restriction.</cite>
Weaknesses: Long-context agent sessions involving many files at once can slow down noticeably on consumer-tier cards.
Recommended hardware: A 24GB GPU such as an RTX 3090 or 4090, or a Mac with 32GB+ unified memory.
Who should use it: Developers who want a genuine coding agent — not just a chat assistant — that can read a repository, plan a multi-file change, and execute it, entirely offline.
Which Model Fits Your Situation
Beginners learning to code are usually better served starting with a cloud assistant, simply to avoid hardware setup friction. If you do want to go local, DeepSeek-Coder V3 (Distilled) is approachable and light enough for modest hardware.
Python developers working on everyday scripts and small projects will likely get the most consistent results from Qwen3-Coder-Next, given its balance of reasoning and coding accuracy.
Web developers who frequently debug from screenshots or work with UI layouts should look at Gemma 4 31B IT QAT for its multimodal support.
AI engineers chasing the strongest open-weight benchmark numbers should evaluate GLM-4.7 Thinking, keeping in mind the tooling ecosystem is still maturing.
Enterprise projects with strict data-residency or compliance requirements are well suited to Devstral Small 2, given its permissive license and genuinely agentic design.
Low-end PCs without a dedicated GPU should stick with DeepSeek-Coder V3 (Distilled) or wait until upgrading, since most models on this list assume real VRAM.
High-end GPU owners with 24GB or more have the most freedom here — Qwen3-Coder-Next, Devstral Small 2, and Llama 4 Scout all become realistic options.
The best coding model isn’t the one with the highest benchmark. It’s the one that fits your workflow.
What Using One Actually Looks Like
Debugging: Paste the failing function and the error message, and ask the model to explain what’s happening before asking for a fix — this catches cases where the model misunderstands the actual cause.
Imagine you’re debugging a Python API.
Instead of writing:
“Fix this code.”
Try asking:
“Explain why this endpoint returns None before suggesting a fix.”
When the model explains the problem first, you’re far more likely to understand the bug instead of just copying the solution.
Learning Python: Ask the model to explain a concept, then immediately ask it to generate three small variations of the same example, so you can compare and test each one yourself.
Writing documentation: Feed the model a function and ask for a docstring plus a short usage example, then verify the example actually runs before trusting it.
Refactoring: Give the model one function at a time rather than an entire file, and review each change before applying it — even strong local models occasionally suggest changes that alter behavior subtly.
Understanding unfamiliar code: Ask the model to summarize what a file does in plain language first, before diving into line-by-line questions. This surfaces its own misunderstandings early, before you’ve built on top of a wrong assumption.
None of these workflows are magic. Local models, like their cloud counterparts, occasionally produce confident, incorrect answers — verifying suggested code before trusting it matters just as much l
